title: Why the healing path includes losing people who cannot follow
author: Kalesh
excerpt: I remember the exact moment I realized I couldn't save everyone. I was sitting on my kitchen floor, back against the cabinet, phone in my hand. A message from...
metaDescription: I remember the exact moment I realized I couldn't save everyone. I was sitting on my kitchen floor, back against the cabinet, phone in my hand. A message...
htmlContent:
I remember the exact moment I realized I couldn't save everyone. I was sitting on my kitchen floor, back against the cabinet, phone in my hand. A message from someone I loved sat there, half-typed, waiting for me to fix something that wasn't mine to fix. And I just couldn't do it anymore.

That's when the grief hit. Not relief. Not freedom. Just this heavy, hollow ache in my chest. Because here's the thing: healing isn't just about learning to stay. It's also about learning to leave. And that part nobody warns you about.

You start doing the work. You read the books. You sit with your body. You learn about your nervous system. You start setting boundaries. And then you look around and realize the people who were with you at the beginning aren't there anymore. Some of them walked away. Some of them you had to let go. And some of them just quietly faded, like smoke through your fingers.

That's what I want to talk about today. The part of healing that feels like a second loss. The part where you grieve relationships that couldn't survive your growth. Because if you're on this path, you've probably felt it too. That strange loneliness that comes with getting better. That quiet mourning for the people who couldn't come with you.

The nervous system knows when someone is no longer safe

I spent years trying to hold onto people who were actively hurting me. Not because I was weak. Not because I didn't know better. But because my nervous system was wired for connection at any cost. That's what survival does to you. It teaches you that safety means keeping people close, even when they're the ones causing the danger.

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, talks about this. He says our nervous system is constantly scanning for cues of safety and danger. It's called neuroception. It happens below conscious awareness. Your body knows, long before your mind catches up, whether someone is safe to be around.

But here's the problem. When you've grown up in chaos, your neuroception gets scrambled. You start feeling safe with people who are unpredictable. You start feeling anxious with people who are stable. Your body has learned that familiar equals safe, even when familiar is actually harmful.

So when you start healing, your nervous system starts recalibrating. That friend who always drains you? Your body starts sending signals. That family member who dismisses your pain? Your gut starts clenching. That partner who can't meet you where you are? Something shifts inside you. And suddenly, staying feels worse than leaving.

That's not a failure. That's your body finally telling the truth. Wild, right? After years of ignoring the signals, your nervous system starts speaking up. And the first thing it says is usually some version of "this isn't safe anymore."

Grieving the relationships that cannot survive your growth

I lost a close friend about two years into my healing journey. We had been through everything together. Bad relationships, career changes, late night phone calls about existential dread. She was there when I was a mess. And I thought that meant she'd be there when I started getting better.

But she wasn't. The more I healed, the more distant she became. The more I set boundaries, the more she accused me of changing. The more I talked about my nervous system and my trauma responses, the more she rolled her eyes. And eventually, she just stopped calling.

I spent months trying to understand what I did wrong. I replayed every conversation. I analyzed every text. I convinced myself that if I had just explained better, or been more patient, or tried harder, she would have stayed.

But here's what I finally understood. Some people are only comfortable with you in your pain. Your growth threatens them because it asks them to look at their own unhealed places. Your boundaries feel like rejection because they've never learned to respect anyone else's. Your healing reminds them that they're still stuck. And that's too uncomfortable for them to sit with.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, talks about how trauma lives in the body. He says healing isn't about getting rid of the trauma. It's about completing the incomplete survival responses that got frozen in your system. And one of those incomplete responses is the ability to leave. To walk away from something that's not safe. To end a relationship that's no longer serving you.

I had to learn that leaving someone doesn't mean you don't love them. It means you love yourself more. And that's a hard truth to swallow. Especially when you've been taught that love means sacrifice, that connection means losing yourself, that loyalty means staying no matter what.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link). Pete Walker's work on emotional flashbacks and the inner critic gave me language for what I was experiencing. It helped me understand that the grief I felt wasn't weakness. It was my system finally allowing me to feel what I had been numbing for years.

Sit with that. The grief you feel when you lose someone on this path is real. It's valid. And it's also a sign that you're actually healing. Because you can't heal and stay the same. And neither can your relationships.

Boundaries are not rejection, they are protection

I used to think boundaries were walls. Cold, hard, impenetrable things that kept people out. I thought setting a boundary meant I didn't care. I thought saying no meant I was being selfish. I thought protecting my peace meant I was abandoning everyone else.

That's the trauma talking. Trauma teaches you that you don't get to have limits. Trauma teaches you that your needs don't matter. Trauma teaches you that love is conditional on your availability, your compliance, your willingness to disappear.

But boundaries aren't walls. They're doorways. They tell people how to be in relationship with you. They say "I want you here, but here's what I need to feel safe." And some people can't handle that. Some people only know how to relate to you when you have no boundaries at all.

A practical guide to boundaries that many find accessible is Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link) by Nedra Glover Tawwab. She breaks down why boundaries feel so hard and how to actually implement them in real relationships. Not the theory of boundaries. The practice. The messy, uncomfortable, necessary practice.

When I started setting boundaries, I lost people. That's just the truth. Some of them got angry. Some of them got quiet. Some of them acted like I had betrayed them by finally having a limit. And for a while, I thought I was doing something wrong. I thought if I was really healing, the relationships would just magically get better.

But here's what I know now. Not everyone is supposed to come with you. Some people were only meant to be with you for a season. Some people were teachers, not lifelong companions. Some people showed up to mirror your wounds, not to witness your healing.

That doesn't make them bad people. It just means the relationship has reached its natural end. And that's okay. That's more than okay. That's part of the process.

Your healing is not a negotiation

I spent years trying to get permission to heal. I wanted people to understand. I wanted them to validate my choices. I wanted them to say "yes, you're doing the right thing." But that's not how healing works. Your healing is not a group project. It's not a democracy. You don't need a committee to approve your boundaries.

Janina Fisher, who specializes in treating complex trauma and structural dissociation, says that healing requires us to become the protector we never had. That means making decisions that other people might not like. That means prioritizing your nervous system over someone else's comfort. That means choosing yourself, even when it costs you relationships.

And it will cost you. That's the part nobody puts on the inspirational Instagram posts. Healing is lonely sometimes. It's lonely because you start seeing things differently. You start noticing patterns you used to miss. You start feeling things you used to numb. And the people who haven't done that work can't meet you there.

A book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link) by Lindsay C. Gibson. It helped me understand why I kept trying to get love from people who weren't capable of giving it. It helped me see that my need for their approval was keeping me stuck in relationships that were never going to be what I needed.

Here's the hard truth. Some people will never understand your healing. Some people will never validate your choices. Some people will never apologize for how they hurt you. And you have to be okay with that. Not because it doesn't hurt. But because your healing doesn't depend on their understanding.

You can grieve the loss of a relationship and still know it was the right choice. You can love someone and still walk away. You can miss who they were and still accept that they can't be that person anymore. That's the complexity of healing. It's not about getting closure from other people. It's about finding closure within yourself.

The grief is part of the growth

I don't want to pretend this is easy. It's not. Losing people on the healing path is one of the hardest things I've ever done. There are days I still wonder if I made the right choice. There are nights I still cry over relationships that ended years ago. There are moments I still wish I could go back to the way things were, even knowing that way wasn't working.

But I also know that I can't unlearn what I've learned. I can't go back to the person who didn't know she deserved better. I can't pretend that my nervous system doesn't know the truth. I can't unfeel the safety I've found in my own company, in my own boundaries, in my own healing.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link) by Deb Dana. She offers tools for regulating your nervous system, especially when you're in the middle of grief or relational loss. It helped me stay grounded when the loneliness felt overwhelming. It reminded me that my body could be a source of safety, even when the world around me felt unstable.

So if you're in this place right now. If you're grieving someone who couldn't follow you. If you're wondering if the healing is worth the loss. Let me tell you this. It is. It is worth every tear, every sleepless night, every uncomfortable conversation. Because the other option is staying small. Staying stuck. Staying in relationships that keep you from becoming who you're meant to be.

You are not wrong for outgrowing people. You are not selfish for protecting your peace. You are not abandoning anyone by choosing yourself. You are simply honoring the truth that your healing matters. And the people who are meant to be in your life will meet you there. Not in your pain. Not in your compliance. But in your wholeness.

That's the hope I hold onto. Not that everyone will stay. But that the ones who do will be the ones who truly see me. And that's worth the loss of everyone else.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link) - A practical guide to boundaries that many find accessible
  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link) - One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors
  • Anchored (paid link) - A practical guide to working with your nervous system
  • Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link) - A book that many find validates their childhood experience
